required.title: [Book] The Complete Cookbook for Young Chefs
required.author: America's Test Kitchen Kids

suggested.title: [Book] Ratio: The Simple Codes Behind the Craft of Everyday Cooking
suggested.author: Michael Ruhlman

supplementary.title: [Book] An Everlasting Meal: Cooking with Economy and Grace
supplementary.author: Tamar Adler

competence_markers.description: The student demonstrates proper knife grip and guide hand technique consistently
The student can perform basic cuts (dice, mince, julienne) to a reasonable standard of evenness
The student can name and execute at least six cooking techniques (saute, sear, roast, braise, simmer, blanch) and explain when each is appropriate
The student can explain the three macronutrients, their caloric values, and their roles in the body
The student can plan a week of dinners, create a shopping list, and estimate cost
The student prepares at least two household dinners per week to standard — on time, complete, with cleanup
The student can adapt recipes based on available ingredients, scaling, or dietary needs
The student can read a recipe and identify the techniques being used, not just the steps listed

cross_connections.description: B1 provides the general nutrition foundation; 2.1.4 builds the performance-specific layer for athletes. The student who understands macronutrients here will find 2.1.4's sport-specific nutrition immediately accessible.
Cooking is applied chemistry. The Maillard reaction, caramelization, emulsification, fermentation — these are chemical processes you perform in the kitchen daily.
Food budgeting is a major household expense. The student who plans meals with cost awareness is building financial competence alongside cooking competence.
Two dinners per week, on schedule, to standard. This is discipline in its purest domestic form.
Scaling recipes, calculating cost per serving, measuring in fractions — cooking is applied math.

content.why_this_matters:
You can make simple meals. Now it is time to actually learn to cook.

There is a difference between someone who can follow a recipe and someone who understands what they are doing in a kitchen. The recipe follower can make a meal when they have instructions. The cook can walk into a kitchen, look at what is available, and produce something good. The cook understands why you sear meat before braising it, why you salt pasta water, why you let dough rest. They know techniques, not just steps.

This packet teaches you cooking as a real skill — technique, vocabulary, knife work, heat management, timing, and the nutritional understanding that turns random cooking into deliberate feeding. By the end, you will not just make meals. You will understand how cooking works, plan meals for a week, and know what your body needs from food and how to provide it.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Knife skills** — safe, efficient cutting technique: the grip, the guide hand, basic cuts (dice, mince, julienne, chiffonade)
2. **Cooking technique vocabulary** — saute, braise, roast, simmer, blanch, deglaze — understanding what each technique does and when to use it
3. **Heat management mastery** — knowing when to use high versus low heat and why, reading the visual and audible cues of cooking food
4. **Meal planning** — planning meals for a full week, creating a shopping list, budgeting time
5. **Nutrition fundamentals** — macronutrients, micronutrients, caloric needs, what a balanced diet actually looks like
6. **Recipe adaptation** — modifying recipes based on available ingredients, scaling up or down, substituting
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Knife Skills

The knife is the most important tool in the kitchen. Learning to use it properly — safely, efficiently, and with control — is a foundational skill that makes every other cooking task faster and better.

**The grip:** Hold the knife with your thumb and index finger pinching the blade just above the handle (the "pinch grip"). The remaining three fingers wrap around the handle. This grip gives you control. Most beginners hold the knife like a hammer with all fingers on the handle — this gives power but no precision.

**The guide hand:** Your non-knife hand holds the food. Curl your fingers into a claw shape — fingertips tucked under, knuckles forward. The flat side of the blade rests against your knuckles as you cut. Your knuckles guide the knife. Your fingertips are never exposed to the blade. This is the technique that prevents cuts.

**Basic cuts:**
- **Rough chop** — irregular pieces, roughly the same size. For soups and stews where appearance does not matter.
- **Dice** — even cubes. Small dice (1/4 inch), medium dice (1/2 inch), large dice (3/4 inch). Even size means even cooking.
- **Mince** — very fine chop. For garlic, herbs, ginger. The pieces should be tiny.
- **Julienne** — thin matchstick strips. For stir-fries, salads, garnish.
- **Chiffonade** — thin ribbons of leafy herbs or greens. Stack the leaves, roll them tight, slice across the roll.
- **Slice** — even flat pieces. Consistent thickness is the goal.

**Practice:** Knife skills improve only through repetition. Dice ten onions and you will be competent. Dice a hundred and you will be good. Start slowly — speed comes naturally as your muscle memory develops. Never sacrifice control for speed.

### Part 2: Cooking Techniques

Every cooking method is a combination of heat type (dry or wet), temperature (high or low), and time (fast or slow). Understanding these variables means you can cook anything.

**Dry heat, high temperature:**
- **Saute** — small pieces of food, hot pan, small amount of fat, constant movement. Fast cooking that develops flavor through browning.
- **Sear** — larger pieces (steaks, chicken breasts), very hot pan, minimal movement. The goal is a brown crust (the Maillard reaction — the chemical process that creates the flavors of browned food).
- **Roast** — oven heat surrounding the food. Higher temperature (400-450F) for crispy exteriors. Lower temperature (300-350F) for gentle, even cooking.
- **Grill** — direct heat from below. Similar to searing but with flame and smoke.

**Wet heat, lower temperature:**
- **Simmer** — liquid at a temperature just below boiling. Small bubbles rising gently. Most soups, sauces, and stews cook at a simmer, not a boil.
- **Braise** — sear first for flavor, then cook slowly in liquid at low temperature. This is how tough cuts of meat become tender — the long, slow heat breaks down connective tissue. Braising is patient cooking.
- **Blanch** — briefly cook in boiling water, then immediately transfer to ice water. Used for vegetables — sets the color and partially cooks without making them mushy.
- **Poach** — cook gently in liquid that is barely simmering. For eggs, fish, delicate foods.
- **Steam** — cook over (not in) boiling water. Gentle, retains nutrients, good for vegetables and fish.

**Combination and finishing:**
- **Deglaze** — after searing, add liquid (wine, stock, water) to the hot pan to dissolve the flavorful browned bits stuck to the bottom. This is the foundation of most pan sauces.
- **Reduce** — simmer a liquid until some of it evaporates, concentrating the flavor. A thin broth becomes a rich sauce through reduction.

### Part 3: Nutrition Fundamentals

You need to understand what food does for your body — not at an expert level, but well enough to feed yourself and others intelligently.

**Macronutrients — the big three:**
- **Protein** (4 calories per gram) — builds and repairs tissue. Meat, fish, eggs, dairy, beans, lentils, nuts. Your body needs protein daily. A meal without protein leaves you hungry sooner.
- **Carbohydrates** (4 calories per gram) — primary energy source. Grains, bread, pasta, rice, potatoes, fruits, vegetables. Not all carbs are equal: whole grains and vegetables provide sustained energy and fiber; refined sugar and white flour provide quick energy and little else.
- **Fat** (9 calories per gram) — essential for brain function, hormone production, and nutrient absorption. Olive oil, butter, nuts, avocado, fatty fish. Fat is not the enemy — your body needs it. But it is calorie-dense, so portions matter.

**Micronutrients:** Vitamins and minerals your body needs in smaller amounts. You get these from eating a varied diet — especially vegetables, fruits, and whole foods. If your diet includes a wide range of real foods (not processed substitutes), you are likely getting adequate micronutrients without supplementation.

**Calories:** A unit of energy. Your body needs a certain number of calories per day to function — roughly 1,600-2,400 depending on age, size, and activity level. Eating more than you need consistently means weight gain. Eating less consistently means weight loss. This is thermodynamics, not opinion.

**The practical rule:** A balanced meal has protein, vegetables, and a starch/grain. It uses real ingredients, not heavily processed substitutes. If you eat this way consistently — not perfectly, but consistently — you are feeding yourself well.

### Part 4: Meal Planning

Cooking one meal at a time is reactive. Planning meals for a week is a system. Systems are more efficient, less wasteful, and produce better results.

**Weekly meal planning process:**
1. **Check what you have.** Look in the refrigerator, freezer, and pantry. What needs to be used before it spoils? Start there.
2. **Plan 5-7 dinners.** Not every meal needs to be planned — breakfast and lunch can be simpler and more flexible. But dinner is the anchor meal, and planning it prevents the 5 PM panic of "what are we eating?"
3. **Create a shopping list** from your meal plan. Organized by store section (produce, meat, dairy, dry goods) to make shopping efficient.
4. **Consider leftovers.** A roast chicken on Monday becomes chicken salad on Tuesday and chicken soup on Wednesday. Planning for leftovers reduces waste and cooking time.
5. **Budget awareness.** Know roughly what groceries cost. A week of meals for a family of four can range from $75 to $250+ depending on choices. Understanding cost-per-meal helps you plan realistically.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Knife Skills
**Developing well:** Pinch grip is automatic. Guide hand is consistent. Pieces are reasonably even. The student works at a controlled pace without rushing. They maintain technique even when tired or distracted.

**Developing slowly:** The student reverts to a hammer grip when not reminded. Guide hand fingers are extended rather than curled. Pieces are wildly uneven. They rush.

**Diagnostic questions:** How much practice are they getting? Knife skills require hundreds of repetitions. If the student chops vegetables only during their two weekly dinners, that is not enough volume. Consider adding a daily prep task — "Your job is to prep the vegetables for dinner every night this week."

### 2. Technique Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can name the technique they are using and explain why. "I am searing this because I want a brown crust — that is the Maillard reaction." They adjust heat based on what the food is doing, not just what the recipe says.

**Developing slowly:** The student follows recipe steps mechanically. They cannot explain why they are doing what they are doing. They do not adjust when something is clearly wrong (food burning, liquid not simmering).

**Diagnostic questions:** Are they reading *Salt, Fat, Acid, Heat*? The principle-based understanding Nosrat provides is what bridges the gap between recipe-following and cooking. If they are only using recipe books, add the Nosrat reading.

### 3. Nutrition Knowledge
**Developing well:** The student can look at a meal and identify the macronutrient sources. They make conscious choices about including protein and vegetables. The nutrition log shows awareness and honest assessment.

**Developing slowly:** The student can recite definitions but does not connect them to actual eating. Their meals remain unbalanced despite knowing the framework.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the nutrition content being taught in isolation or connected to actual cooking? Every dinner they prepare is an opportunity to discuss: "What is the protein here? Where are the vegetables? Is this balanced?"

### 4. Meal Planning and Responsibility
**Developing well:** The student plans independently, shops from a list, cooks on schedule, and cleans up. The meals are complete and reasonably good. The household can rely on them for their assigned meals.

**Developing slowly:** Plans are incomplete or unrealistic. Shopping is forgotten until last-minute. Meals are late, incomplete, or the kitchen is left messy.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the expectation clear and enforced? If the student's dinner night slides without consequence, they learn that the responsibility is flexible. It is not. Hold the standard — the meal is ready at the expected time, period.
